title: Northern Renaissance in the fifteenth century (part 1)
topic_path: A beginner's guide to the Northern Renaissance
Northern Renaissance in the fifteenth century (part 1)

assessment_items.question_content:
**This reliquary was a gift for Jean Duc de Berry and would have highlighted $\_\_\_\_\_\_$.**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0ce96a71bec990e23d36a4ad0cac9da2.jpeg)

*Holy Thorn Reliquary* (made in Paris for Jean, Duc de Berry), c. 1390s, gold, enamel, ruby, pearl, sapphire, and rock crystal, 30 x 14.2 x 6.8 cm (British Museum, London)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: This reliquary was a gift for Jean Duc de Berry and would have represented the piety and political power of the recipient.

choices.content: the piety and political power of the recipient
the luxurious and indulgent life of the recipient
the ordination and priesthood of the recipient
the military and political power of the recipient

assessment_items.question_content:
**The *Holy Thorn Reliquary* shows a scene of $\_\_\_\_\_\_$**.

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0ce96a71bec990e23d36a4ad0cac9da2.jpeg)

*Holy Thorn Reliquary* (made in Paris for Jean, Duc de Berry), c. 1390s, gold, enamel, ruby, pearl, sapphire, and rock crystal, 30 x 14.2 x 6.8 cm (British Museum, London)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The reliquary shows a scene of Christ and the apostles at the Last Judgment.

choices.content: St. Helena Discovering the Crown of Thorns

hints: Medieval people used relics to pray to the associated saint.

choices.content: as a point of access to the divine.
as the cornerstone for a new religious building.
as jewelry in order to be closer to God.
in burials in order to enter heaven.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Sluter and de Werve's *Mourners* from the *Tomb of Philip the Bold* are $\_\_\_\_\_\_$**.

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0e6c875dbbdf0a037933fe96eb9e9a3a.jpeg)

Claus Sluter (with Claus de Werve), *Mourners, Tomb of Philip the Bold*, Duke of Burgundy, 1410 (Museum of Fine Arts, Dijon)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Sluter and de Werve's *Mourners* from the *Tomb of Philip the Bold* are weighty, individualized, and emotionally expressive.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is NOT true of Claus Sluter and Claus de Werve's *Tomb of Philip the Bold*?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The figures of the Mourners occupied an arcade underneath a sculptural effigy of the Duke.

choices.content: The alabaster carvings show Carthusian monks, clergy, and family mourning Philip the Bold, Duke of Burgundy.
The figures of the *Mourners* originally decorated the walls of the funerary chapel.
The figures are individualized and express emotion, which is typical of the work of Claus Sluter.
These figures are weightier and more realistic than earlier slender and swaying Gothic figures and show a change consistent with art made during the Renaissance.

assessment_items.question_content:
**The prophets and angels display emotion which, according to the speakers, was intended to inspire prayer.**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ae5a7b3c44f064434011067fb9cb03b9.jpeg)

Claus Sluter (with Claus de Werve), *The Well of Moses*, 1395-1405 (calvary finished 1399, prophets 1402-05, painted by Jean Malouel c. 1402),

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Sluter and de Werve's *Mourners* from the *Tomb of Philip the Bold* are weighty, individualized, and emotionally expressive.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Sluter's prophets $\_\_\_\_\_\_$.**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ae5a7b3c44f064434011067fb9cb03b9.jpeg)

Claus Sluter (with Claus de Werve), *The Well of Moses*, 1395-1405 (calvary finished 1399, prophets 1402-05, painted by Jean Malouel c. 1402),

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Sluter's prophets emerge from their niches, instead of being confined by the niches, engage with the viewer, and show individualized faces.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Carthusian monks who lived at Chartreuse de Champmol lived apart from the community and dedicated themselves primarily to $\_\_\_\_\_\_$**.

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d30c4a4e4954f7b8aeadd3b1fe4f4d1e.jpeg)

Claus Sluter (with Claus de Werve), Well of Moses, 1395-1405 on the grounds of the former Chartreuse de Champmol, a Carthusian monastery in Dijon, France established by Philip the Bold, Duke of Burgundy.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: These Cathusian monks lived apart from the community and dedicated themselves to solitary prayer.
